---
title: "Draft: covariance structure of the residuals"
output: html_document
bibliography: REFERENCES.bib
---

<!--
Standalone draft for review. Written to slot into the `#### Residuals`
subsection of `sec:math-glm`, directly after the z-residual (Eq.
\@ref(eq:z-residual)). Same notation and conventions as the current
section; no matrix notation. Citations carry page numbers (PDFs read
in full: Cook & Weisberg 1982 p. 14; Das & Imon 2016 p. 10; Kozak &
Piepho 2018 p. 12).
-->

#### Covariance structure of the residuals

The model errors $\varepsilon_i$ in Eq. \@ref(eq:glm) are mutually
independent with constant variance $\sigma^2$. Their observable
counterparts, the raw residuals $e_i$ (Eq. \@ref(eq:raw-residual)),
inherit neither property: estimating the $k$ parameters imposes $k$
linear constraints on the $N$ residuals, so the residuals are by
construction heteroscedastic and mutually correlated, even when the
errors are not [@Cook:1982, p. 14].

The variance of an individual residual is

\begin{equation}
\operatorname{Var}(e_i) = \sigma^2 (1 - h_i),
(\#eq:var-leverage)
\end{equation}

where the leverage $h_i \in [0,1]$ measures how strongly the predictor
values of observation $i$ pull its own fitted value $\hat{y}_i$;
high-leverage observations have smaller residual variance
[@Cook:1982, p. 14]. Two residuals covary according to

\begin{equation}
\operatorname{Cov}(e_i, e_j) = -\,\sigma^2 h_{ij}, \qquad i \neq j,
(\#eq:cov-leverage)
\end{equation}

with $h_{ij}$ the leverage shared between observations $i$ and $j$; the
correlation is negative and vanishes only when $h_{ij} = 0$. The diagonal
case reproduces the variance above, $h_{ii} = h_i$, so both equations use
one leverage symbol.

Two consequences follow for the residual diagnostics:

- The z-residual (Eq. \@ref(eq:z-residual)) divides by the single
  global standard error $SE_\text{res}$ and therefore leaves both the
  leverage-dependent variance in Eq. \@ref(eq:var-leverage) and the
  correlation in Eq. \@ref(eq:cov-leverage) unchanged. Dividing instead
  by the individual standard error gives the internally studentised
  ("standardised") residual
  $r_i = e_i / \sqrt{SE_\text{res}^2\,(1 - h_i)} = z_i / \sqrt{1 - h_i}$,
  which removes the leverage term in Eq. \@ref(eq:var-leverage) but not
  the correlation in Eq. \@ref(eq:cov-leverage). Standardised residuals
  are therefore preferred over raw residuals for assumption checking
  [@Kozak:2018, p. 12; @Cook:1982, p. 14].
- Because each residual is a linear combination of all errors, its
  distribution is closer to normal than that of the errors themselves
  ("supernormality"). A normality test applied to residuals is
  correspondingly conservative: it tends to under-reject non-normality
  of the errors [@Das:2016, p. 10].

# References {.unnumbered}
